133452.explanation: We invest a lot of energy in finding good municipal council members with ties to Helmond. We also support citizen assemblies and all forms of participation. We are not against a referendum, but prefer to engage in dialogue with residents rather than opting for a vote without nuance.

133454.explanation: The municipality incurs costs for providing dog exercise areas and emptying dog waste bins. These costs are covered by dog taxes. We believe it is fairer if only dog owners pay these costs.

133455.explanation: We want more business parks in Helmond that provide varied employment and do not cause nuisance to residential neighborhoods. Nuisance-causing companies should be actively excluded.

133456.explanation: With the new heat law, residents can start small heat or electricity cooperatives themselves. These are great initiatives that contribute to reducing grid congestion and sustainability.

133457.explanation: The municipality must fulfill its legal duty to reserve housing for refugees with a residence permit. This concerns only a few homes per year.

133458.explanation: Integration of newcomers is very important to ensure everyone can participate as quickly as possible, for example through work. This saves money in the long term. A lot of funding is already available. But if more money is needed and well justified, we will provide it.

133459.explanation: Employers are responsible for decent housing, but work and living must be sufficiently separated. No one should be left homeless immediately after losing their job.

133460.explanation: We believe it is fairer for those who produce more waste to contribute more to the costs. But people with little money or no car should be supported in waste collection.

133461.explanation: Fireplaces and wood stoves produce fine particulate matter which affects people with lung complaints. Therefore, we support a burning ban, but only during orange and red alerts (i.e., calm wind conditions) from the RIVM.

133462.explanation: To stimulate biodiversity, it is important to mow the grass less frequently during certain times of the year, but at other times it can be mowed more often.

133463.explanation: Bulky waste should be picked up free of charge for people who have no help in their network to remove it. Free removal for everyone would be a significant cost that would either lead to cutbacks or an increase in municipal taxes.

133464.explanation: Playing sports at a club is good for physical and mental health, but for low-income people it can be a bridge too far among all other fixed expenses. We would like to offer discounts on sports clubs for low-income residents through a HelmondPass.

133466.explanation: The housing shortage is very large, and for a diverse supply, social rental homes are needed alongside owner-occupied and some higher-end rental homes.

133467.explanation: For young people who want to live independently, a room can be a nice first step to independent living. Also for people who want to stay temporarily in Helmond for work.

133468.explanation: In Helmond, we encourage participation in society. We preferably help people find employment, but volunteer work is also an option.

133469.explanation: These measures help prevent that people with low incomes risk falling below the poverty line due to municipal taxes.

133470.explanation: Informal caregivers have a heavy task and do important unpaid work. This also applies to some volunteers who work many unpaid hours per week. These are good reasons not to apply for jobs during this period.

133472.explanation: A livable and green city center is an incentive and attracts shops and shoppers. This is good for the local economy and a vibrant center. However, we want sufficient parking spaces at the edges of the center and for disabled citizens.

133473.explanation: We want businesses to be helped in clarifying their financial situation and finding the best solution for their circumstances.

133474.explanation: Freedom of choice can contribute to good treatment by healthcare providers. Trust arises with a click, and then the offered care is most effective.

133477.explanation: We find it important that residents know how the municipality uses algorithms. Helmond already does this. When choices made by algorithms affect people, a human must always be involved in the decision.

133478.explanation: The municipality must primarily communicate in Dutch, but if necessary and when all Helmond residents must be reached, for example in the interest of children, other languages are also an option. We encourage newcomers to learn Dutch.

133479.explanation: In procurement, we reward parties that show good employment practices. Job security is part of this, alongside responsible wages and good working conditions.

133480.explanation: It is good to remain critical of laws and regulations, especially for voluntary organizations. Rules must serve a purpose, such as guaranteeing safety for event visitors.

133481.explanation: The housing shortage is too great, and Helmond residents have also been waiting a long time for housing. Doing more than legally required is not a good choice given the current situation.

133483.explanation: Climate change is a major threat to us all. We also experience more heat and heavy rain showers in Helmond. We believe we should contribute to becoming climate neutral, and if that can be achieved by 2050, we certainly want to do so.

133484.explanation: Nature-inclusive means that we take nature into account with all interventions in public spaces.

133485.explanation: We often talk with young people inside and outside our party and hear that they feel there are too few places where they can meet each other.

133486.explanation: Good charging infrastructure is important, especially for people without their own driveway. We want to expand this based on the needs of each neighborhood. There must be a minimum number so that, besides residents, visitors can also charge their cars.

133487.explanation: Preserving nature and sustainability are both important goals that require careful consideration. Preferably, we do not want to sacrifice agricultural land for solar panels, but there are good examples of dual use where solar panels coexist with agriculture.

133488.explanation: Being able to be who you are and safely express that is an important foundation for freedom. It is important that the local government shares this as well.

133490.explanation: Making music is a way for young and old to express themselves, belong somewhere, and have fun. We make this possible for children from low-income families through the Leergeld Foundation.

133491.explanation: Internships are also very important for vocational (MBO) students to complete their education properly. The municipality encourages companies to offer internship opportunities.

133492.explanation: The use of cameras that recognize faces is prohibited by law and is a major infringement of personal freedom.

133493.explanation: It is important that residents can reach enforcement officers (BOAs) well and that BOAs let people know what they have done with a report. But this does not have to be 24 hours a day.

133494.explanation: From all the expert information we have received, it appears that a second coffee shop does not bring positive changes in Helmond. If a decision is made to establish a second coffee shop, we will again listen to residents, school boards, youth workers, and the police and make a decision based on that.

133496.explanation: We support communal living forms that match people's desire to share more. Communal living helps reduce loneliness and makes caring for each other easier.

133497.explanation: The vast majority of social rental homes go to people with ties to Helmond under the current allocation rules, and we agree with this.

133498.explanation: It is important that housing allocation is transparent and that there is a clear point of contact for complaints and discrimination.

133499.explanation: It is necessary to set up a housing corporation together with Eindhoven for mid-rent housing. There are many people who earn too much for social rent but too little to buy or rent in the private sector.

133500.explanation: In a time of severe housing shortage, it is good to encourage that homes are used for their primary function: living.

133501.explanation: Housing construction rules are not always disadvantageous. With specific rules for Helmond, deviations are possible, for example, for building assisted living units and splitting homes. But we are not in favor of additional rules that lead to more bureaucracy.

133502.explanation: In Helmond, a housing coach is active who helps people with all matters related to moving. We do not believe that all seniors should receive a moving allowance because many seniors can easily pay for a move themselves. A moving allowance, in our view, should only apply to seniors who cannot afford a move.

133503.explanation: In the municipality of Helmond, we use the threshold of 130% to qualify for support for various schemes. This is urgently needed given rising costs. We wish people a life, not just survival.

133504.explanation: Poverty is a major problem in Helmond. We tackle it at the root with early detection to prevent and quickly address debts. This works best close to people in the neighborhood.

133505.explanation: During the renovation of the Kasteel-Traverse, careful attention was paid to improving traffic flow, also for cyclists and pedestrians. In time, we want to downgrade the Kasteel-Traverse, but only if the traffic situation can handle it without too much nuisance for surrounding streets.

133507.explanation: Driving only 30 km/h in the city is much better for quality of life. It is safe, cleaner, and causes less noise.

133508.explanation: Public transport in Helmond is not optimal. We have four train stations but too few buses. Public transport has also become very expensive. As a party, we believe transport is a basic need and should be free. Therefore, we support investigating how more public transport can be made available in Helmond and whether it can be free for residents with low incomes.

133511.explanation: The history of slavery is part of our history, but unfortunately, labor exploitation still occurs worldwide today. We want not only attention to the history of slavery but certainly also more attention to modern slavery.

133452.explanation: The VVD wants residents to think along early through genuine participation and clear consultation, not just afterward through a referendum. Referendums take a lot of time and money and make decisions less predictable. The municipal council is elected to make decisions and explain them.

133454.explanation: The dog tax is outdated; many surrounding municipalities have already abolished it. The VVD wants to reduce unnecessary burdens for residents and chooses fair and understandable local tax policy.

133455.explanation: Business parks provide jobs and economic growth. If there is demand, Helmond must offer space for entrepreneurs to invest and grow. The VVD chooses future-proof business parks with good accessibility, so employment in the city is maintained.

133456.explanation: We welcome generating sustainable energy together, but not every initiative automatically requires municipal support. The VVD chooses tailored support: assistance where it contributes to feasible, affordable sustainability and benefits the neighborhood, without new standard mandatory subsidies.

133457.explanation: Helmond takes its legal responsibility for housing status holders. However, the VVD does not consider it right to structurally give them priority on social rental housing. Scarcity is high, and homes must be distributed fairly with consideration for neighborhood support.

133458.explanation: Integration is important, but it’s about what works, not spending more money. The VVD wants to focus on effective measures such as learning the language and quickly entering the workforce. Extra budget without clear results helps no one forward.

133459.explanation: Employers who attract labor migrants must also take responsibility for good housing. This way, we prevent extra pressure on the housing market and nuisance in residential areas. The VVD chooses clear agreements and decent housing, with oversight and enforcement.

133460.explanation: Submitting waste properly must be easy. Free access to the recycling center prevents illegal dumping and keeps neighborhoods clean. The VVD opts for a practical solution, with attention to cost control and efficient waste policy.

133461.explanation: The VVD wants clean air, but a general ban on wood stoves and fireplaces goes too far. Many residents burn responsibly. We prefer education and enforcement in case of nuisance instead of a patronizing ban.

133462.explanation: Good maintenance of public greenery contributes to livability and safety in the neighborhood. Overgrown grass gives a messy impression and can cause nuisance. The VVD chooses a well-maintained public space, with attention to nature and a neat street view.

133463.explanation: Waste must be easy and affordable to dispose of. That is why we encourage free use of the recycling center and existing collection moments. Unlimited free bulky waste collection at home makes waste policy expensive and less sustainable. The VVD opts for a practical balance.

133464.explanation: We want no one to be left out. Therefore, we lower financial barriers to sports, also for adults with limited means. We do this targeted and practical (e.g., through arrangements with clubs), not automatically by fully paying for everything.

133466.explanation: We must build enough affordable housing, including social rental. But a fixed minimum of 35% in every plan is too rigid and can delay construction. The VVD chooses customization based on Helmond’s housing needs: social rental where needed, as well as mid-rent and affordable ownership.

133467.explanation: The VVD wants to firmly tackle nuisance and abuses caused by subdividing homes, but a blanket ban is too harsh. We opt for tailored enforcement where livability is at risk, and space for appropriate room rental where it can be responsible.

133468.explanation: Anyone receiving benefits who is able to do so must provide an appropriate contribution to society. This increases involvement and job opportunities. The VVD stands for participation, with customization and attention to everyone’s situation.

133469.explanation: Remission (debt forgiveness) is necessary for residents who truly cannot pay. The VVD wants to prevent people from getting further into trouble. We remain vigilant against abuse and keep schemes focused and affordable.

133470.explanation: Informal care is very valuable and deserves support. At the same time, participation according to ability remains important. The VVD chooses customization: where informal care is intensive, the job application obligation can be adjusted, but not automatically for everyone.

133472.explanation: An attractive inner city requires good accessibility. Car-restricted measures must not come at the expense of entrepreneurs, visitors, and accessibility for the elderly and families. The VVD chooses balance: pleasant staying and good accessibility.

133473.explanation: Entrepreneurs temporarily in trouble deserve a second chance. Debt counseling that retains the business prevents bankruptcies and saves jobs. The VVD chooses customization and perspective, not unnecessary write-offs.

133474.explanation: Freedom of choice in healthcare is important but must not lead to fragmentation or exclusion. The VVD opts for good, accessible care for everyone, with room for personal preferences where appropriate, always starting from quality and equality.

133477.explanation: Residents have the right to clear explanations when the municipality uses algorithms. Transparency prevents errors and distrust. The VVD wants a modern, reliable municipality that explains how decisions are made in clear language.

133478.explanation: The VVD believes the municipality should principally communicate in Dutch; that is the official language and helps integration. At the same time, the municipality must be able to assist residents in exceptional situations when language is a barrier (for example, through personal contact or extra support). Therefore, we do not choose “only” or “always” but for customization.

133479.explanation: The VVD values good employment conditions but does not want to impose extra procurement requirements and rules. The municipality should primarily focus on quality, reliability, and price within the law. This way, we keep SMEs and good freelancers fairly visible.

133480.explanation: Events provide liveliness and pride. The VVD wants fewer unnecessary rules and one clear point of contact so organizers know quickly what to expect. Safety remains important, but the municipality should help make things possible rather than obstruct.

133481.explanation: Helmond fulfils its legal responsibility for shelter but no more than required. The VVD wants a fair distribution among municipalities and shelter that matches capacity and safety. Extra shelter without conditions undermines support and implementation.

133483.explanation: The VVD wants sustainability in a way that remains feasible and affordable. 2050 is the nationally agreed target. Moving faster may lead to higher costs for residents and entrepreneurs. We choose realistic steps that work, without extra local compulsion.

133484.explanation: The VVD wants to speed up building and keep homes affordable. Mandatory nature-inclusive requirements make building more expensive and slower. Where possible, we stimulate green initiatives but without extra local obligations on top of national rules.

133485.explanation: Young people need safe places to meet and be active. That prevents nuisance and offers structure. The VVD opts for targeted meeting places and activities, preferably with supervision and jointly with the neighborhood.

133486.explanation: More and more residents drive electric and must be able to charge even without a private driveway. The VVD wants to accelerate public charging points where there is demonstrable demand, so neighborhoods remain accessible and future-proof, without unnecessary rules or delay.

133487.explanation: The VVD chooses the roof-first principle: solar panels belong on roofs, business buildings, and noise barriers. We protect nature and agricultural land. This way we make sustainability smart and affordable, without unnecessarily covering our landscape.

133488.explanation: Everyone must be able to feel safe and free in Helmond. The VVD stands for equality and against discrimination. Participating in Pride is a visible signal that everyone belongs, without compromising safety or other priorities.

133490.explanation: Music lessons are valuable, but the VVD does not support a general municipal payment obligation. Through Stichting Leergeld and targeted schemes, we already ensure that children from low-income families can participate without new costly obligations.

133491.explanation: An internship is essential for a good start in the labor market. The VVD wants the municipality, schools, and companies to cooperate to provide sufficient internship places. This prevents study delays and ensures talent can start working in Helmond.

133492.explanation: The VVD does not exclude new technology outright if it can contribute to safety. The use of facial recognition may only happen under strict conditions, proportionally, and with strong privacy safeguards. Safety and freedom must remain balanced.

133493.explanation: Nuisance and insecurity do not keep office hours. The VVD wants that enforcement officers (boa's) are reachable in the evenings and weekends as well, so reports are acted on faster. This increases safety and trust in the neighborhood.

133494.explanation: The VVD is cautious about expanding coffee shops. Our priority is safety, livability, and preventing nuisance. With good enforcement and clear agreements, one coffee shop is sufficient. Expansion without necessity and support is unwise.

133496.explanation: Multigenerational homes can be a good addition in larger new-build plans, but demand varies by neighborhood and project. We want to stimulate this where there is demand but not impose a rigid obligation that unnecessarily delays or increases building costs.

133497.explanation: Social rental is scarce. The VVD believes that Helmond residents with ties to the city-living, working, or studying-should have a fair chance. Within the law, we choose a transparent and fair allocation so local residents are not displaced.

133498.explanation: Tenants must be helped easily in cases of abuses. This can be done via a rental team but also by strengthening existing counters and cooperation with partners. For us, what matters is that support is well arranged; the form is chosen based on effectiveness and costs.

133499.explanation: Mid-rent housing is badly needed, but establishing a new housing corporation is a large and risky tool. The VVD opts first for faster building and agreements with existing corporations and market parties. Only if that proves insufficient do we consider cooperation or new forms.

133500.explanation: We want to tackle vacancy. A vacancy tax is not our first choice; we start with measures that work faster (accelerating repurposing, cutting rules, addressing owners). If that has insufficient effect, we do not rule out additional incentives.

133501.explanation: Helmond must build faster. Extra local rules make homes more expensive and delay projects. The VVD chooses national standards and less bureaucracy so we can realize affordable homes faster without unnecessary stacks of requirements.

133502.explanation: A relocation allowance can help seniors make the move to a suitable home. This frees up a family home for first-time buyers and families. The VVD opts for measures that speed up flow and get the housing market moving.

133503.explanation: Help must reach those who really need it. By lowering the threshold to 120%, we focus support on the lowest incomes and keep schemes affordable and targeted. This threshold also aligns with percentages used by surrounding municipalities.

133504.explanation: People with financial worries must be helped close by and quickly, especially in vulnerable neighborhoods. This can be through a neighborhood desk, but also through neighborhood consultation hours or support in existing neighborhood facilities. We choose the form that is most effective and does not cause unnecessary extra organizational costs.

133505.explanation: The Kasteel-Traverse is an important traffic artery. Fewer lanes or exits without a good alternative leads to traffic jams and detours through residential neighborhoods. The VVD chooses accessibility and safety: first a full alternative, then adjustments.

133507.explanation: 30 km/h is often logical in residential areas and around schools, but not as a general standard for almost the entire built-up area. The VVD chooses customized solutions: 30 where needed for safety, and 50 km/h on (multiple) main and access roads with safe design and good traffic flow.

133508.explanation: We want everyone to be able to get around, even on a limited budget. But free public transport is expensive and can compromise quality and availability. The VVD opts for affordable and reliable public transport, with targeted support or discounts where necessary.

133511.explanation: We tackle discrimination and exclusion here and now. History deserves attention, but the municipality does not need to set up extra programs around the slavery past. The VVD chooses education, equal opportunities, and an inclusive city, focusing on what works today.

133452.explanation: Helder Helmond wants to bridge the gap between residents and the municipality and give residents more influence. So openness, transparency, and administrative renewal, direct participation and say to increase trust in local politics.

133454.explanation: The dog tax is outdated and unfair. Dogs are often a permanent and valued part of the family and reduce loneliness. We do not want to punish that.

133455.explanation: Entrepreneurs in Helmond are indispensable for employment, innovation, and vibrancy. To retain and grow them, Helder Helmond chooses extra business parks. This creates more jobs close to home.

133456.explanation: Helmond Climate Neutral by 2030 is unachievable. Helder Helmond values sustainability, but we stand for realistic climate policy and honest communication about what is possible and what is not. We especially focus on insulating homes.

133457.explanation: No, Helmond residents who have been waiting for a home for years must be prioritized first. Helder Helmond wants current residents of Helmond to have precedence over refugees, speculators, or expats.

133458.explanation: Helder Helmond does not want to spend extra money without clear results. Participation in society is important, but people also have their own responsibility to integrate.

133459.explanation: Employers who employ labor migrants must provide good housing themselves. Not at the expense of residential areas.

133460.explanation: This lowers the threshold for residents to dispose of waste properly and helps prevent illegal dumping. This way we keep the city clean and make it easier for residents to do the right thing.

133461.explanation: Residents must take each other into account, especially regarding nuisance and health. At the same time, a blanket ban on open fireplaces and wood stoves goes too far. Not everything is solved by bans. We choose common sense, mutual respect, and tailored solutions.

133462.explanation: Green spaces and streets just need to be properly maintained and residents’ complaints should be handled quickly and seriously. Helder Helmond wants a city that is clean, green, and livable. Weeds are rampant, paving stones are loose, and reports remain unresolved for too long. That is unacceptable.

133463.explanation: Helder Helmond believes that bulky waste collection at home should be free or at a low cost. This helps residents and prevents illegal dumping. Residents also have their own responsibility, but the municipality must facilitate this well and organize it simply.

133464.explanation: Sports should be possible for everyone, including adults with limited funds. Moving and participating is important for health and well-being. At the same time, it must not become an unlimited subsidy pool. Help is intended for people who really need it. Where that is the case, the municipality is ready to support.

133466.explanation: We must primarily build affordable homes. They do not have to be social rental homes. Starters, families, and seniors also need affordable ownership and mid-level rent. Fixed percentages such as 35% social rental do not help with that. Each project should be assessed for what is truly needed.

133467.explanation: Rooming (multiple occupancy) can cause nuisance, pressure on neighborhoods, and fewer family homes. At the same time, a general ban is too rigid. We choose clarity and tailored solutions: intervene where rooming causes problems, but allow space where it is responsible and well organized.

133468.explanation: Everyone who can, should contribute to society. Meaningful social work can help people stay involved and maintain structure. Not everyone can do the same, and for people with care duties, health problems, or other limitations, there must be space and understanding.

133469.explanation: Remission of debt should only be applied under strict and clear rules. Aid must reach people who really need it. So that residents with demonstrable financial problems do not get into further trouble.

133470.explanation: Informal care must be valued. People who care for a family member or acquaintance perform important work. But paid work is also important, including for one’s own perspective. This requires less pressure and a humane attitude from institutions, with attention to what someone can handle at that moment.

133472.explanation: The car is part of Helmond. Accessibility and traffic flow are important for residents, entrepreneurs, and visitors. Making more streets car-reduced in the city center is therefore not the right approach. We choose a city that remains easily accessible to everyone.

133473.explanation: Helder Helmond believes entrepreneurs with debts should be helped with tailored solutions. The goal is recovery, not closure. Where possible, the company should be preserved so people can move forward again.

133474.explanation: No, freedom of choice is important, but care must above all remain good, feasible, and affordable. The municipality must ensure good care for everyone without leading to extra costs, waiting lists, or complicated execution.

133477.explanation: If the municipality uses algorithms for benefits, allowances, and minimum income policies, residents must understand how those systems work and what consequences they have for them. Transparency and clear explanations are necessary to prevent errors, distrust, and injustice.

133478.explanation: Communicating in Dutch helps integration and places responsibility with residents themselves. We certainly consider people with visual or hearing impairments, low literacy, and other neurological challenges, and provide appropriate support where needed.

133479.explanation: This must primarily benefit Helmond companies. That strengthens the local economy and creates involvement with the city. Where possible, we opt for companies with employees on permanent contracts for quality and continuity.

133480.explanation: Events create atmosphere, encounters, and pride. Unnecessary local rules must be removed so associations and organizers can organize events more easily. The municipality should think along and facilitate, not hinder.

133481.explanation: Helmond cannot currently accommodate asylum seekers. Helmond already has enough problems with safety, livability, housing shortage, and care. We must solve those first. First stabilize and secure our neighborhoods, then consider possibilities. Accepting more asylum seekers than legally required is not responsible now.

133483.explanation: 2035 is not feasible; 2050 is a guideline. Even that may be ambitious, but we want to seriously commit to it, with attention to affordability and support from residents. Goals must be realistic and achievable.

133484.explanation: No, mandatory nature-inclusive building means extra rules, higher costs, and further delays in housing construction. We especially need fast and affordable homes. Green is important but should not unnecessarily make building for Helmond residents difficult and expensive.

133485.explanation: Good community centers and other meeting places improve livability and ensure that young people (and elderly) can meet each other in a positive way. This helps prevent problems and strengthens the neighborhood.

133486.explanation: An electric car is a personal choice and the municipality does not have to pay for that. Just as we do not build gas stations, the municipality does not need to install extra charging points in every neighborhood.

133487.explanation: Solar panels belong on roofs and not in nature or on farmland. This protects the landscape and preserves space for housing, nature, and agriculture.

133488.explanation: We stand for respect and equality. Community initiatives, such as Pride Week, deserve support and space. However, active participation or an organizing role by the municipality goes too far for us. The municipality can facilitate where needed but should not take center stage.

133490.explanation: Culture and talent development should not depend on the wallets of parents. At the same time, support should be targeted to children who really need it, so it is not a general scheme but real help.

133491.explanation: The municipality has a large network of schools and companies and can easily bring these parties together. This way, vocational students can find internships without extra costs.

133492.explanation: Facial recognition in public spaces must not become the norm. The privacy of residents is paramount. At the same time, there must be room to use this method only when truly necessary and under strict conditions.

133493.explanation: Municipal officers must be accessible and approachable in our city. Direct contact with enforcers, every day of the week, contributes to safety and increases residents' trust. And reduces the burden on the police.

133494.explanation: A second coffeeshop creates distribution and reduces street dealing. The monopoly creates an undesirable monopoly. More choice and better supervision reduce illegal trade and pressure on public spaces, improving safety and livability in Helmond.

133496.explanation: Helder Helmond does not want more, but fewer rules. Multi-generational homes can be valuable, but making them a mandatory requirement in large new construction projects does not help the building process. Each plan must be assessed to determine what is necessary and appropriate for the neighborhood.

133497.explanation: Our city has been built by Helmond residents themselves. By people who live and work here, keep associations running, look after their grandchildren, and care for their parents. These are the people who make Helmond what it is. They deserve priority.

133498.explanation: Tenants should not be left alone when facing problems. There must be a strong tenant team that actively supports tenants with complaints about their home or landlord, clearly siding with the tenant. This ensures fair relations and better housing quality.

133499.explanation: Cooperation can be useful, but Helmond has sufficient scale to effectively manage mid-rent policy on its own, tailored to our city and its residents.

133500.explanation: Vacant homes, especially those owned by housing corporations, are unacceptable in times of housing shortage. We urgently need these homes. A vacancy tax helps to make homes available more quickly for people waiting for a house.

133501.explanation: We want fewer rules and faster housing construction. The national regulations are sufficient; additional municipal requirements, especially regarding climate, make building unnecessarily expensive and slow. That does not help housing seekers.

133502.explanation: Seniors only move when good, suitable homes are available that are not more expensive than their current residence. The focus must be on that: ensuring enough affordable and appropriate housing for seniors, so that natural turnover occurs.

133503.explanation: Policy should help, not create new barriers for residents who are just getting by. Lowering the threshold causes many struggling people to fall just outside support. It is precisely these borderline cases who deserve assistance.

133504.explanation: This assistance already exists in Helmond. The focus should be on making existing provisions easier to find and access, rather than setting up new counters.

133505.explanation: As long as there is no fully fledged alternative, the Kasteel-Traverse must remain functional. Fewer lanes and exits will cause problems elsewhere. Freight traffic can be reduced, but the road must retain its function until a good alternative is available.

133507.explanation: In large parts of Helmond, 30 km/h is already the standard. More important than new rules is that 30 km/h zones are well designed so people actually comply and traffic safety genuinely improves.

133508.explanation: The quality and reliability of public transport are currently too poor. Free public transport then helps no one. First, public transport must become better and more reliable; only afterwards can support for specific groups be considered.

133511.explanation: Helmond has hardly played a role in this past and certainly not the residents living here now. The municipality should focus on today’s and tomorrow’s problems instead of continuously emphasizing the past.

133452.explanation: A consultative referendum is less effective than a good participation process. Ultimately, the municipal council decides. The SP supports a binding corrective referendum; that actually allows residents to overturn decisions.

133454.explanation: The dog tax in Helmond is a designated tax whose proceeds are intended for facilities such as off-leash and dog walking areas, etc. So it benefits dogs and dog owners. If abolished, all residents will have to contribute to these facilities.

133455.explanation: Projections for Helmond’s need for industrial sites for 2023-2041 show a space demand of 32 to 65 hectares. A diverse range of sites ensures attractive jobs for residents of all education levels.

133456.explanation: The SP supports strengthening local energy cooperatives and resident initiatives. We want residents to participate more easily, become co-owners, and share in the profits of energy projects in their neighborhoods.

133457.explanation: The municipality has a legal task to fulfill. A ban on priority for status holders in social housing conflicts with the equality principle in Article 1 of the Constitution.

133458.explanation: Integration is very important. The minimum language level must be B1 and apply to everyone. We must speak the same language to live together and combat segregation. People must also be helped into work or otherwise contribute.

133459.explanation: Many labor migrants have an employer who is also their landlord. If they lose their job, they also lose their home. Until The Hague prohibits this, the municipality must protect labor migrants from this exploitation.

133460.explanation: This directly leads to an increase in the waste disposal levy for all households. Instead of the polluter paying, everyone pays.

133461.explanation: That is the goal. Wood smoke is unhealthy for everyone. It contains many harmful substances. Children, elderly, and people with respiratory complaints can be especially affected. When the burn indicator shows orange/red, burning must be prohibited.

133462.explanation: Less mowing means more biodiversity, flower-rich verges, and natural pest control (like caterpillars). It helps combat urban heat and creates habitats for insects and other animals. Verges directly alongside roads and at intersections are mowed more frequently for traffic safety.

133463.explanation: This has major financial and logistical consequences. Calculations estimate costs around 4.4 million euros. To cover these costs, the waste levy for our residents would have to be significantly increased, so it is not 'free'.

133464.explanation: Sports and exercise contribute (preventively) to the wellbeing and health of our residents. Sport and exercise must be accessible and available to everyone, in the broadest sense.

133466.explanation: The standard is 30% for projects of 50 or more homes. Increasing up to 35%-40% in specific neighborhoods. These are neighborhoods with currently few social rental homes. The goal is to build neighborhoods with a mix of (expensive) owner-occupied and social rental homes.

133467.explanation: Renting a room to a student as a host family should be possible. Renting out entire homes per room as a slum landlord is undesirable.

133468.explanation: The Participation Act is implemented based on trust and tailor-made approaches. People are supported and helped, not obligated.

133469.explanation: Residents with low incomes must be supported so they do not (further) get into trouble. If debt remission is removed, they will face great financial pressure with all its consequences.

133470.explanation: The Participation Act is implemented based on trust and tailor-made approaches. Informal caregivers must be spared and supported.

133472.explanation: The city center must become low-traffic. Due to the mobility transition, cars will gradually become less dominant and more streets must be low-traffic. This creates a safer, healthier living environment with space for greenery, play, meeting, and tranquility.

133473.explanation: Entrepreneurs in Helmond struggling with debts or financial problems can use specialized support via the municipality and regional partners.

133474.explanation: People with a different background or socioeconomic position are at higher risk of misdiagnoses or poorer treatment. Discrimination and unconscious biases also play a role. More attention is needed for culturally sensitive work in care and the social domain.

133477.explanation: Municipalities must be able to explain how a decision was made. Citizens have the right to know if an algorithm played a role in the decision-making. This measure should prevent incidents like those in the childcare benefits scandal from recurring.

133478.explanation: Municipal information in multiple languages is crucial for accessibility, equal opportunities, and effective service delivery, because it enables all residents (including low-literate people, newcomers, and those with a different mother tongue) to understand their rights and duties.

133479.explanation: Companies are not directly forced to work only with permanent staff. However, procurement conditions focus on quality labor relations and social impact. Companies that can demonstrate social and sustainable operations have an advantage in contract awards.

133480.explanation: Rules must guarantee the safety of visitors and the surrounding area. Custom solutions must be possible without abandoning all carefully established rules. The municipality must ensure that (potential) event organizers are well guided and supported.

133481.explanation: The distribution law ensures asylum seeker reception is fairly allocated and every municipality takes responsibility-no more and no less. Asylum seekers receive humane shelter in Helmond.

133483.explanation: We must pursue the goals as much as possible but justly. Everyone should be able to participate and improve without life becoming unaffordable. The SP’s goal is to realize a climate-neutral and climate-just society by 2040.

133484.explanation: Nature-inclusive building contributes to more biodiversity, climate adaptation, and a healthier living environment for humans and animals. It integrates nature not outside but within the built environment for a more sustainable and healthier future.

133485.explanation: Young people must be able to think and talk along themselves. It should not be politics but the young people themselves who indicate what they need and what they require for that.

133486.explanation: Anyone driving electric should be able to charge fairly and affordably anytime and anywhere. For this, a nationwide network of public charging points must be established. No profit model for companies, but a reliable service, accessible and affordable for everyone.

133487.explanation: The SP mainly wants to realize solar energy via solar panels on roofs, so existing space is optimally used and nature and agricultural land are spared. This should be organized collectively and publicly so that the benefits go to residents and not to commercial parties.

133488.explanation: Participation shows that the municipality welcomes everyone, contributes to acceptance at schools and work, and calls attention to existing discrimination, including in other countries where LGBTI+ rights are violated.

133490.explanation: Music education is not a luxury but a valuable investment in the overall development of a child, connecting head and heart and can stimulate lifelong curiosity and enjoyment. Music lessons must be accessible to all children.

133491.explanation: Every young person deserves an internship place to truly learn a profession.

133492.explanation: The use of facial recognition in public spaces is in principle prohibited. It infringes on privacy and algorithms often show biases based on race, gender, or age, which can lead to wrongful accusations and discrimination.

133493.explanation: This is undesirable and unworkable. Objections include the safety and privacy of the public order officers (boa) and interference with task execution. Unlike the police, a boa does not have a legal assistance task. For direct nuisance, the police should be called.

133494.explanation: Research has shown that there is no direct need or necessity for a second coffee shop. Should this change in the future, we are open to it.

133496.explanation: Housing must be built for all target groups. There is a shortage of various types of homes, especially for starters, families, and seniors. Multi-generational homes make it possible to keep a mix of young and old in the city, where young people have a future and older people can continue to live comfortably.

133497.explanation: Although the intention is to protect and strengthen the Helmond community, the main disadvantages are increased waiting times for others, legal and ethical questions about discrimination, and the risk that the 'ordinary' Helmond resident is left out.

133498.explanation: Tenant teams in municipalities are essential for protecting tenants against excessive rents, overdue maintenance, and abuses by 'slumlords'. Tenant teams can operate effectively as independent foundations subsidized by the municipality.

133499.explanation: Helmond and Eindhoven collaborate with existing housing corporations and tenant organizations to build more affordable homes, including mid-priced rentals. The focus is on a coordinated approach and increasing the supply of mid-priced rentals instead of a joint housing corporation.

133500.explanation: Houses are meant to live in. Vacancy must be addressed.

133501.explanation: Local regulations complement national rules because municipalities are best able to assess what is specifically needed in their environment. This is established in the Environmental Act, where national building technical requirements are combined with local customization in the municipal environmental plan.

133502.explanation: The SP is in favor, provided this is voluntary and the compensation is paid from national legislation, so it does not have to come at the expense of other provisions in Helmond.

133503.explanation: The limit of the affordability standard remains 130% for a wider reach of minimum income groups. Due to rising living costs, people with an income up to 130% of the minimum experience similar financial problems as the previous target group at 100-120%.

133504.explanation: The SP finds it important that people with financial problems are helped as quickly and well as possible. This works best close to people's own environment. Therefore, there must indeed be help at the neighborhood level, which fortunately is already organized this way in many neighborhoods.

133505.explanation: Several exits will be removed to improve traffic flow and make the city center less car-intensive. The final design aims at a redesign with more space for greenery and adjusted bike paths, which affects car traffic capacity in the long term.

133507.explanation: More safety, calm, and space for cyclists and pedestrians. Promotes livability.

133508.explanation: The SP supports free public transport for people with low income. However, this is a provincial/national matter; municipalities have insufficient resources and are not responsible for public transport.

133511.explanation: The apologies were a good start but concrete follow-up steps are needed. Such as structural education, memorial places, and more awareness of the impact of this past on current society, with attention to lasting effects such as racism. There is a need for a long-term vision.

133452.explanation: We prefer participation through targeted programs.

133454.explanation: We adhere to the polluter pays principle. However, the proceeds must be used for dog walking areas, etc.

133455.explanation: The problem is that little land is available anymore. More regional cooperation can offer solutions.

133456.explanation: Part of the solution to the energy problem lies there.

133457.explanation: After all, the municipality has a statutory task to fulfill.

133459.explanation: Where possible, the municipality should cooperate.

133460.explanation: Is unaffordable. Cost price should be passed on.

133461.explanation: We cannot prohibit it. We can, however, make air filters mandatory.

133464.explanation: Other institutions are available for that.

133467.explanation: In this way, 'room renting' can be prevented.

133468.explanation: That strongly depends on the capabilities of the person involved.

133469.explanation: The government is often the biggest cause of financial problems. This way, the municipality can provide some relief.

133470.explanation: Caring for a close relative is also a contribution to the community.

133472.explanation: You cannot completely get residents out of their cars. Car policy must be balanced. Making areas car-free is not a solution for that.

133473.explanation: Entrepreneurs already have a hard time. Especially small businesses must be able to receive support.

133474.explanation: This comes with a price tag. The municipality must provide sufficient supply options.

133477.explanation: Many people don't know about this, so just do it.

133478.explanation: Residents should be proficient in the language. If that is not the case, we must offer courses.

133479.explanation: You can't just impose that as a government.

133480.explanation: The regulatory burden is already far too high.

133481.explanation: The region must also take its responsibility.

133483.explanation: We believe this is a realistic goal.

133485.explanation: That has really been a forgotten group in recent years. That can be much better.

133486.explanation: This is absolutely a requirement to keep up with the growth of electric cars.

133487.explanation: Strongly agree. The appearance of nature has not improved.

133488.explanation: Shows that everyone counts in the community.

133490.explanation: Music education can encourage participation in the community. Music lessons should not become elitist.

133491.explanation: The municipality can and must be a driver in this area.

133492.explanation: If you have nothing to hide, that is not a problem.

133493.explanation: Completely agree. This way, involvement and problem-solving can be made more visible.

133494.explanation: One shop is more than enough.

133496.explanation: There is a need for that.

133497.explanation: That increases the involvement of residents with the city.

133498.explanation: Housing associations must provide for that. It is not a task of the municipality.

133499.explanation: Helmond is big enough to manage on its own.

133500.explanation: The question is only whether that is legally sustainable. Still a supporter.

133501.explanation: The regulatory burden is already high enough.

133502.explanation: It must remain reasonable. It can be an incentive to move.

133503.explanation: It is sufficient as it is now.

133504.explanation: Tackling the problem close to the source can save a lot of trouble. Prevention is better than cure.

133505.explanation: Practically not feasible. It would be ideal, but the city is no longer set up for that. It is not doable.

133507.explanation: That strongly depends on the local situation. We cannot state that like this.

133508.explanation: That is unaffordable. Moreover, the municipality does not have authority over that. There are other alternatives for cheap transport. Automaatje etc.

133511.explanation: We believe that the legacy of slavery is terrible but must be placed in its historical context. The current government cannot be held responsible for something that happened 250 years ago.

133452.explanation: D66 considers citizen participation important. This can be done, among other ways, via a referendum, provided it is well framed and has a clear topic choice.

133454.explanation: D66 is not fundamentally for or against, but believes that dog tax should be spent on dog walking areas, cleaning up dog feces, and safety and enforcement. Those with a dog can contribute to these costs incurred by the municipality through this tax.

133455.explanation: Due to employment and location in the Brainport region, it is necessary to realize new industrial estates, such as at Varenschut. But we also want to make efficient use of space. Therefore, we will also start restructuring our existing business parks.

133456.explanation: Tomorrow's energy must be clean and affordable. D66 Helmond wants an energy transition that belongs to all of us. With a local energy community, fair prices, and reliable facilities, so everyone can participate in a sustainable future. Network congestion must also be taken into account.

133457.explanation: This priority is necessary because the municipality receives a biannual quota from the national government for the number of refugees with residence permits to be housed. Without priority, a municipality cannot meet this quota. It also reduces pressure on the asylum centers.

133458.explanation: D66 wants to start teaching the Dutch language and civic integration in emergency shelters, assess diplomas, and remove work limitations. In this way, refugees can contribute to society faster. The current budgets for this are sufficient.

133459.explanation: Both for existing businesses and the allocation of new industrial estates, we want entrepreneurs to pay attention to good housing and language classes for employees. Preferably by decoupling living and working. For seasonal workers, living on their own property temporarily can be allowed.

133460.explanation: Processing waste and incinerating residual streams costs a lot of money. This is not free, and therefore D66 believes it is important that those who bring the waste also bear the costs, not the entire community.

133461.explanation: Although a wood fire can be cozy, residents increasingly experience nuisance and health complaints. It is also bad for air quality (fine dust). Nevertheless, D66 first focuses on awareness, good information, and guidance via local rules. In the long term, wood burning should decrease.

133462.explanation: Mowing less is better for biodiversity. There should also be room for customization: mowing more often in places where it is necessary (playgrounds, sports fields, for traffic safety and to prevent deterioration), but mowing less in parks, verges, and nature strips.

133463.explanation: Processing waste and incinerating residual streams costs a lot of money. This is not free, and therefore D66 believes it is important that those who provide the waste also bear the costs, not the entire community.

133464.explanation: D66 stands for equal opportunities for everyone, regardless of background and family income. However, we also support an integral approach to support measures for low-income groups. For adults in sports, we see alternatives: for example, exercising in public spaces, in groups, or on public sports equipment.

133466.explanation: In accordance with the Housing Agreement, we align new construction with the mix of housing in the neighborhoods as set out in the housing agreement (30% social rental and 70% affordable). As also described in Helmond's Housing Vision, this can be customized per neighborhood.

133467.explanation: Given the demand for housing, D66 focuses not only on new construction but also on more intensive use of existing homes, such as shared housing, splitting, flexible living, and friends living. This obviously requires customization, and a permit must be applied for for subdividing houses.

133468.explanation: D66 supports guidance and opportunities that help people participate again in society. Socially meaningful work can help with this. However, a person must be capable of this, and the work must be appropriate. A broad view should also be taken on what is socially meaningful.

133469.explanation: D66 is against stopping the waiver of municipal taxes for low incomes, because this contributes to poverty reduction and equal opportunities.

133470.explanation: D66 supports (temporary) exemption from the job application requirement for informal caregivers receiving welfare benefits. This concerns customization for caregivers with an intensive care task.

133472.explanation: Because this contributes to a livable and attractive city. Car-restricted does not mean car-free: you can reach the center by car, but it can be parked in an underground parking garage. This leaves more public space available.

133473.explanation: D66 supports entrepreneurs receiving debt assistance without immediately losing their business, because this protects jobs, economic activities, and entrepreneurship.

133474.explanation: D66 supports freedom of choice in care that takes into account religion, culture, or belief, as long as this remains fair and feasible within the existing moral boundaries and capacity within care. This should also not lead to exclusion of care providers.

133477.explanation: D66 believes the municipality must be transparent about algorithms used for benefits and allowances so that residents maintain trust and control. This also aligns with the Algorithm Register of the Dutch Government.

133478.explanation: D66 believes that communication should be in Dutch, as the official language. Only in exceptional cases can communication be in another language, which requires customization. We also assume that with the latest technology, residents are able to make their own translations.

133479.explanation: D66 believes municipal work should preferably be performed by companies with permanent employees to ensure quality, continuity, and social security.

133480.explanation: D66 supports fewer regulations for organizing events, as long as safety, livability, and the environment are adequately maintained. When adding rules, it should be considered how to keep the extra burden on initiators as low as possible.

133481.explanation: D66 supports that Helmond shelters asylum seekers in accordance with the distribution law. It is humane to shelter people in need. If all municipalities act in accordance with the distribution law, there is no need to take in more asylum seekers.

133483.explanation: D66 believes Helmond must strive for climate neutrality by 2035, because speed is crucial for the climate and innovation. This ambition as a guiding principle helps to accelerate Helmond's sustainability efforts.

133484.explanation: Nature-inclusive construction (e.g., bird boxes, green roofs) promotes sustainability, biodiversity, and quality of life. This does not delay the construction process because developers and contractors can already incorporate this early in the design. It also concerns national regulations.

133485.explanation: D66 supports creating more meeting places and activities for young people because this strengthens involvement, development, and livability.

133486.explanation: D66 supports adding more charging stations in every neighborhood because this promotes sustainable mobility and the energy transition. Of course, network congestion and existing parking needs must be taken into account. Cable ducts near private homes can also contribute to this.

133487.explanation: D66 believes new solar panels should not be installed in nature reserves. Before solar panels can be placed on agricultural land, the solar charger must first be followed, and D66 supports multiple land use (combining functions).

133488.explanation: D66 supports awareness, inclusiveness, and promoting acceptance. Everyone should be able to be themselves.

133490.explanation: D66 stands for equal opportunities for every child, regardless of background and family income. We believe art and culture should be accessible to everyone. This also argues for a rich school day, where art and culture is a fixed part of the school day.

133491.explanation: D66 finds it important that the municipality, together with schools and companies, provides internships for MBO students because this strengthens their development and the local labor market. This also fits with Helmond's identity as a city of makers.

133492.explanation: D66 believes the municipality should not use automatic facial recognition in public spaces to protect privacy, trust, and proportionality.

133493.explanation: D66 supports residents having direct contact with enforcement officers because this promotes safety, involvement, and quick assistance. However, we also see a role for the police.

133494.explanation: D66 supports the opening of a second coffee shop. The number of coffee shops should match the city’s needs, with the main goal of combating illegality and street dealing.

133496.explanation: D66 supports innovative and flexible living arrangements, including homes classified as multigenerational housing (where multiple generations live together). These homes contribute to a more diverse housing offer, flow-through, and social cohesion and fit changing family compositions.

133497.explanation: Based on national regulations, priority is currently not possible; the share of social rental allocations to residents from Helmond already exceeds 50% (legal limit). Custom measures to help seniors move within their own neighborhood (own network) do contribute to flow-through.

133498.explanation: D66 supports establishing a tenant team because this protects tenants, offers support, and contributes to better housing quality. Helmond already has a Reporting Point in accordance with the Good Landlord Act.

133499.explanation: D66 favors the idea that municipalities jointly establish a corporation for mid-rent housing to promote flow-through in the housing market. Unfortunately, corporations cannot build all mid-rent homes due to external rules, and mid-rent construction is not always picked up by the market.

133500.explanation: D66 supports the introduction of a vacancy tax given the tight housing market. After the Fiscal Compilation Act 2026 takes effect (from January 1, 2026) and changes to the Vacancy Act, it will be easier to introduce a vacancy ordinance and tackle vacancy.

133501.explanation: In Helmond, only one additional requirement applies (about water storage), which is sufficient. National regulations do not allow many additional requirements regarding the construction of new homes. Building codes come directly from national regulations.

133502.explanation: D66 supports that seniors can receive a moving allowance when leaving a large home to promote flow-through, good care in appropriate housing, and livable neighborhoods.

133503.explanation: Since the municipality must cut back due to a significant drop in revenue from the Municipal Fund (the cliff year), 120% is an option, but between 120 and 130% there should still be room for customization.

133504.explanation: D66 supports guiding low-income groups to promote early assistance and social involvement. This should also be addressed by our welfare partners, but this help does not need to be arranged separately for each neighborhood in a neighborhood desk.

133505.explanation: D66 supports the renewal of the Castle Traverse. Fewer exits can enhance safety and livability. However, reducing lanes is currently not possible; this must happen sequentially. So downscale only when research indicates or an alternative replacement route has been realized.

133507.explanation: D66 supports the introduction of 30 km/h zones for safety and livability in neighborhoods, except for main roads.

133508.explanation: Since the municipality must cut back due to a significant drop in revenues from the Municipal Fund (the cliff year), it is important to limit costs. Unfortunately, the municipality cannot bear all costs itself and would like to tackle this together with partners, organizations, and entrepreneurs.

133511.explanation: D66 supports apologies from the municipality and the lectures, celebrations, and commemorations that are already being held. This helps raise awareness. But beyond that, we would like the national government to take up this responsibility and for initiatives supported by society to emerge.

133452.explanation: Lokaal Sterk opposes referendums. Residents should be involved beforehand through participation and dialogue. Referendums occur after the fact and often come too late, oversimplify complex choices, and cause more division without truly solving problems.

133454.explanation: The dog tax is used for dog facilities in public spaces. Abolishing it means all residents pay, even those without dogs, which is unfair. Lokaal Sterk supports reforming the tax rate to a fixed and transparent rate per dog.

133455.explanation: A strong economy requires space for entrepreneurs. This means investing in existing industrial areas and targeted expansion. Without enough space, companies and jobs risk leaving. Lokaal Sterk opts for restructuring, sustainability, and expansion to preserve employment.

133456.explanation: Local energy initiatives deserve support if they are feasible, affordable, and widely supported. Municipal support should be temporary and targeted, such as start-up subsidies or loans. No open-ended schemes, but clear frameworks and demonstrable local added value.

133457.explanation: The housing shortage affects starters, families, and seniors. Prioritizing specific groups increases tensions and undermines support. Lokaal Sterk chooses equal treatment. The solution lies in faster construction and better flow, not in redistributing scarcity.

133458.explanation: Integration is important, but extra funding is not automatically the answer. First, it must be clear what works and what doesn’t. Lokaal Sterk opts for result-oriented policy: sharp evaluation, effective use of resources, and no extra spending without demonstrable effect.

133459.explanation: Housing seasonal workers is a shared responsibility, as named by the Roemer commission. Employers take the initiative with good housing, preferably on or near their own site. The municipality sets clear frameworks, supervises quality, livability, and enforcement.

133460.explanation: Free waste disposal does not exist. The costs always end up with all residents. Lokaal Sterk therefore opposes a completely free recycling center and favors a fair system where those who bring more waste pay more and those who separate waste well pay less.

133461.explanation: A general ban on wood burning is unfeasible and unfair. Lokaal Sterk chooses tailor-made measures: action where nuisance occurs, combined with education and enforcement. This addresses problems specifically without unnecessarily restricting all residents.

133462.explanation: A well-maintained public space is important for livability and safety. Lokaal Sterk opts for clear choices: biodiversity where appropriate and more frequent mowing in recreational and residential areas. This prevents untidiness and increases use of green spaces.

133463.explanation: Free bulky waste collection sounds good but leads to higher costs and more waste. Those costs must ultimately be paid and fall on all residents. We therefore choose a fair system: collection for a fee, with tailored solutions for those who cannot dispose of bulky waste themselves.

133464.explanation: Sports promote health and social interaction. Lokaal Sterk chooses to better utilize existing schemes and lower barriers. This way we reach more low-income adults without needing new complicated regulations.

133466.explanation: Stubbornly sticking to 35% social housing can slow or even halt housing construction. Lokaal Sterk therefore opts for flexibility. Sometimes flexibility is needed to keep projects feasible and thus realize homes faster for those urgently seeking housing.

133467.explanation: Lokaal Sterk opposes a general ban on subdividing homes but also opposes uncontrolled growth. In a city with housing pressure, subdivision can add extra living space but only under strict conditions regarding safety, livability, management, and maximum concentration per street.

133468.explanation: Recipients of welfare benefits who are able should contribute something back to society. This provides structure and increases self-worth and involvement. Lokaal Sterk opts for a meaningful reciprocity with tailor-made solutions: those who can, participate; those who truly cannot, are not obliged.

133469.explanation: Debt forgiveness prevents residents with low incomes from becoming further trapped in debt. Abolishing it leads to payment problems, stress, and higher costs in the long term. Lokaal Sterk consciously opts to prevent rather than repair, as this is smarter socially and financially.

133470.explanation: Informal caregivers deliver an indispensable contribution to care in our society. Combining intensive informal care with a job application obligation is often not feasible. Lokaal Sterk chooses to provide space and trust so caregivers can sustain their care tasks.

133472.explanation: Lokaal Sterk is open to greening, but the accessibility of the city center must be strengthened, not restricted. A lively center requires good access for entrepreneurs, residents, visitors, and emergency services. Therefore, we choose to invest in accessibility and traffic flow instead of closures.

133473.explanation: Entrepreneurs can temporarily face problems due to circumstances. Forced closure of viable businesses costs jobs and harms the local economy. Lokaal Sterk believes debt assistance should be accessible while preserving the business where responsible.

133474.explanation: Care that aligns with culture or beliefs increases understanding and communication between caregiver and client. This reduces misunderstandings and unnecessary referrals. We therefore promote freedom of choice within care offerings because appropriate care works more efficiently and helps control costs.

133477.explanation: When algorithms are used in decision-making, residents must be able to understand how choices are made. Without explanation, there is a risk of arbitrariness and distrust. We therefore opt for maximum transparency and explainable governance as prerequisites for a trustworthy digital government.

133478.explanation: Dutch remains the main language. But if residents do not understand crucial information about care, safety, or services, policies fail. Clear communication is therefore essential. We opt for additional effective communication where necessary so everyone is reached.

133479.explanation: If the municipality is only allowed to work with companies with permanent contracts, it becomes harder and more expensive to get work done. This can cause delays, less choice, and higher costs for residents. Lokaal Sterk chooses quality and reliability without unnecessary rules.

133480.explanation: Events bring liveliness, social interaction, and economic activity. Organizers now often get stuck in regulations. Lokaal Sterk wants the municipality to facilitate better and think along, while maintaining safety and clear agreements.

133481.explanation: Asylum reception must remain feasible and manageable. Helmond must comply with obligations from the distribution law but not do structurally more than legally required. Extra reception without sufficient capacity and support strains facilities and undermines trust in governance.

133483.explanation: Sustainability goals must be achievable and affordable. The year 2050 aligns with national and European agreements and maintains support. Lokaal Sterk chooses realistic steps instead of unattainable deadlines.

133484.explanation: Lokaal Sterk supports quality and careful construction but opposes "mandatory" nature-inclusive requirements. Strict obligations raise costs and delay housing construction. Quality is secured through tailor-made solutions and good spatial choices, not rules that block affordable housing.

133485.explanation: Young people need places to meet and build social contacts. Without such facilities, boredom increases and the chance of nuisance grows. Lokaal Sterk invests in youth because strong social networks prevent problems and strengthen the city's future.

133486.explanation: More charging stations are needed to enable electric driving. Lokaal Sterk opts for a demand-driven and neighborhood-focused approach. Additional chargers must not lead to displacement of regular parking spaces. Public space must be fairly distributed among all users.

133487.explanation: Nature and agricultural land are scarce and valuable. Solar panels should primarily be placed on roofs and industrial areas. Lokaal Sterk chooses careful land use and protection of the surrounding countryside.

133488.explanation: Lokaal Sterk stands for equality and respect for everyone. This should be reflected in structural policy and services, not symbolic participation in specific events. Inclusion applies to all residents.

133490.explanation: Music lessons contribute to children's confidence, creativity, and development. Equal opportunities must not depend on parents' income. Lokaal Sterk chooses to better utilize and promote existing schemes so more children can participate.

133491.explanation: Without internships, vocational students cannot complete their education. The municipality must therefore take responsibility by offering internships itself and connecting employers, so youth gain work experience and businesses have future staff.

133492.explanation: Lokaal Sterk believes security tools must remain available within legal frameworks. A general ban on facial recognition hampers effective action in specific situations. Careful use with clear rules and privacy protection is better than excluding everything in advance.

133493.explanation: Nuisance and minor annoyances do not stop during office hours. Enforcement officers must also be available evenings and weekends. Visible and accessible enforcement increases the sense of safety and aligns with residents' daily lives.

133494.explanation: There is currently no demonstrable need for a second coffeeshop. Without clear demand or problem, nothing is solved and unnecessary risks are taken for livability and enforcement. Lokaal Sterk wants objective insight first and clear conditions for location, supervision, and safety.

133496.explanation: Lokaal Sterk sees the added value of multigenerational homes but opposes mandates. Additional requirements increase costs and delay building projects. In a time of housing shortage, focus must be on speed and feasibility to build homes faster for everyone seeking one.

133497.explanation: With scarce social rental housing, it is logical to consider ties to Helmond. People with work, family, or a social network tend to stay here more, know their neighborhood, and contribute to cohesion. This promotes involvement and ensures more stable and livable neighborhoods.

133498.explanation: Tenants sometimes face abuse and do not always dare to take action. A tenant team offers advice and support, strengthens tenants' position, and contributes to a fairer and better functioning rental market.

133499.explanation: Establishing a new corporation takes much time, money, and organization before a single home is built. This does not solve the housing shortage now. Lokaal Sterk therefore opts for faster solutions by better using existing corporations and cooperation with market parties.

133500.explanation: Vacancy is frustrating during a housing shortage, but often has logical causes such as renovation, sale, or legal procedures. A vacancy tax requires a lot of enforcement and barely yields additional homes. Therefore, Lokaal Sterk chooses measures that truly help: building and repurposing.

133501.explanation: Helmond consists of diverse neighborhoods with different construction challenges. Rules that fit one area may cause delays or increased costs elsewhere. That is why local customization is necessary. However, with one clear precondition: regulations must improve building quality, not delay housing construction.

133502.explanation: A moving allowance helps seniors voluntarily move to suitable homes. This prevents expensive social support adjustments and frees up family homes for starters and young families. Lokaal Sterk sees this as a smart measure that accelerates flow and genuinely helps get the housing market moving.

133503.explanation: Those who work extra hours must truly notice it. Work should pay off. Lokaal Sterk chooses clear limits with customization, so that schemes do not abruptly stop and people can gradually grow towards financial independence.

133504.explanation: Help close by prevents money worries from escalating into debts. Neighborhood desks in vulnerable areas are a logical first step, but support must be accessible to all residents who need help, regardless of the neighborhood they live in.

133505.explanation: The Kasteel-Traverse is a crucial traffic artery. Fewer lanes lead to extra pressure, shortcut traffic, and longer travel times. That harms livability and safety. Lokaal Sterk does not support restrictive measures without a fully viable alternative.

133507.explanation: Traffic safety requires customization. Not every road is suitable for 30 km/h. Lokaal Sterk wants to look at each street’s function, design, and use, instead of one general standard for the entire urban area.

133508.explanation: Free public transport via the municipality means all residents pay for it, while far from everyone uses it. That is costly and difficult to justify. Lokaal Sterk chooses fair and responsible policies that remain affordable and do not shift burdens onto others.

133511.explanation: Helmond faces major choices. Lokaal Sterk chooses to look ahead and take responsibility for what residents need NOW: livability, safety, housing, care, and opportunities. That is where the municipality should put its energy and resources to strengthen Helmond’s future.

133452.explanation: Participation is important, but representative democracy remains leading.

133454.explanation: Dogs, and actually cats too, cause nuisance through, among other things, their droppings, sometimes noise nuisance, etc. Dog walking areas need to be created.

133456.explanation: Citizen initiatives accelerate the energy transition.

133457.explanation: Joining at the back of the line.

133458.explanation: A lot of money is already going towards integration. It's the central government's task.

133459.explanation: Employers generate the profits but place housing problems with the municipalities. If you want the benefits, you must also bear the burdens. So provide housing yourself, but according to the municipality’s established criteria.

133460.explanation: Making the economy circular costs money. Recycling centers are facilitated and equipped by the municipalities. If all this were free, it would become unaffordable at some point. Realism is required here.

133462.explanation: Greenery in general also with safety in mind.

133463.explanation: Collection should be done, but not for free.

133464.explanation: Sufficient opportunities already exist for this group to play sports.

133467.explanation: Such a scheme already exists. It is an initiative to tackle housing shortages. But renting to students or to care providers can activate informal care or relieve loneliness.

133468.explanation: Volunteering can lead to re-entry into a real job. In any case, reintegration – becoming employable and actively part of society again – boosts self-worth and confidence.

133469.explanation: Otherwise, people get into trouble more and more.

133470.explanation: Provided a minimum of 20 hours of informal care per week.
